findings.title: Commit step push-failure retry loop swallows failure when no changes exist (early exit 0 inside subshell semantics OK, but loop exits 1 only on last attempt without short-circuit cleanup)
findings.description: The retry loop performs `git pull --rebase --autostash --strategy-option theirs origin main || git rebase --abort || true` before each push attempt. If the rebase fails AND `rebase --abort` succeeds, the chain returns 0 and the loop proceeds to push a HEAD that may be missing remote commits (theirs strategy would normally win, but if the rebase itself bailed out the abort restores pre-rebase state and we push stale local HEAD; with --force-with-lease absent this push will simply be rejected each retry). Because the abort path is masked with `|| true`, the loop can never see the rebase failure and will burn all 3 retries on the same stale state.
findings.mechanism: When `git pull --rebase` hits an unresolvable conflict despite -X theirs (e.g. binary file / submodule add/add), the rebase enters interactive state. `git rebase --abort` returns 0 (success on abort). Local HEAD is restored to pre-rebase state. `git push` is then attempted against a remote that has commits the local HEAD does not contain — push is rejected as non-fast-forward. Loop sleeps and repeats; the same condition recurs because nothing has changed. After 3 attempts the workflow exits 1, but only after wasting ~30s sleep.
findings.reproducer: On 1st of month, monthly-sec-xbrl pushes to main between daily-pull's commit and push. If concurrency group correctly serializes them this is moot — but if a manual push lands between the rebase and push (e.g. someone editing on main), the retry path cannot self-heal.
findings.impact: Daily commit step occasionally hard-fails with `Push failed after 3 attempts`, triggering Discord failure alert and (via `if: success()` gate on Deploy to GitHub Pages step) skipping the Pages deployment — yesterday's report stays visible on gh-pages with no indication that today's data has changed in main.
findings.suggested_fix: Distinguish rebase-success from rebase-aborted: `git pull --rebase ... && git push` and on failure inspect the working tree. Drop the `|| true` after `git rebase --abort` so the failure surfaces. Optionally add `git fetch origin main && git reset --hard origin/main` then re-cherry-pick the local commit as a final fallback.

findings.title: Yahoo Pull step uses continue-on-error: true — empty / corrupted snapshots dir feeds every downstream step
findings.description: `Run Yahoo Pull` has `continue-on-error: true` AND `timeout-minutes: 165`. If the pull crashes (OOM at 6144 MB heap, unhandled rejection, network blowup) the workflow continues with whatever partial state exists in `./snapshots/`. The Verify Pull Coverage step that follows is intended as the gate but reads from `./snapshots/_manifest.json` first, then falls back to file count. If a previous run's manifest is still present (manifest is committed and re-checked-out) the gate uses STALE manifest values while today's `.json` files are missing or partial.
findings.mechanism: Step 1 (Yahoo Pull) crashes early. Manifest from yesterday is still committed at HEAD (line 91 reads './snapshots/_manifest.json'). The comment at line 162 says 'manifest deleted at pull start' but if the crash happens BEFORE pull-yahoo.js reaches its delete-manifest call, yesterday's manifest is still on disk and used to compute the coverage gate. n_ok and n_total reflect yesterday's universe size — gate passes even though today's actual .json files are stale.
findings.reproducer: Manually corrupt pull-yahoo.js to throw at module-load time. Run workflow. Verify Pull Coverage step reads yesterday's manifest, declares pass, and downstream report generation runs against stale snapshot files.
findings.impact: Modes-Report, Picks-History, Methods-History all run against stale snapshots and get committed/deployed as today's output. Backtest vintage continuity is silently broken — picks-history will record yesterday's picks as today's vintage, contaminating walk-forward performance measurements (the very thing comment at line 132-133 says must not happen).
findings.suggested_fix: Either remove `continue-on-error: true` from Yahoo Pull (let it hard-fail), OR add a guard step BEFORE Verify Pull Coverage that deletes any pre-existing _manifest.json so the file-count fallback always engages on partial pulls. Also: check `mtime` of snapshots and reject any older than e.g. 18h.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-CI-003 (concurrency cancellation) and F-CI-006 (no freshness check on inputs to picks-history).

findings.title: Cron overlap risk: daily-pull at 02:00 UTC, monthly-sec-xbrl at 04:00 UTC — daily-pull timeout-minutes: 180 can run past 04:00 UTC
findings.description: Daily pull starts at 02:00 UTC with a 180-minute timeout (max end 05:00 UTC). Monthly SEC pull starts at 04:00 UTC on the 1st. The concurrency group `main-push` with `cancel-in-progress: false` correctly queues monthly behind daily — BUT monthly-sec-xbrl has only 90-minute timeout. If daily-pull on the 1st runs until 04:30 UTC, monthly-sec-xbrl waits in queue ~30 min, then has ~60 min remaining of its own 90-min timeout to actually pull SEC data. SEC XBRL pull is rate-limited (10 req/s) and on >10k tickers takes >60 min, so it will timeout.
findings.mechanism: GitHub Actions concurrency queue holds monthly-sec-xbrl while daily-pull is running. The job-level timeout (90 min) clock starts when the job actually starts running, not when it was queued. So waiting in queue is fine — but daily-pull running long means monthly-sec-xbrl starts late on the 1st of the month, and SEC pull still needs its full duration. The real problem: daily-pull at 02:00 UTC frequently runs 90-120 min, finishing ~03:30-04:00. Monthly cron fires at 04:00. If daily is still running at 04:00, monthly queues. When daily finishes ~04:15, monthly starts with 90 min. Until 05:45. SEC pull-sec-xbrl.js on 12k tickers at 10 req/s = 20 min minimum + retries. Probably fine — but tight.
findings.reproducer: Inspect run history on the 1st of any month; check daily-pull duration vs monthly-sec-xbrl start time.
findings.impact: Possible monthly SEC pull timeout on 1st-of-month when daily-pull runs long. Recoverable via manual workflow_dispatch but represents silent data gap.
findings.suggested_fix: Move monthly-sec-xbrl cron to a clearly non-overlapping window, e.g. '0 8 1 * *' (08:00 UTC, after daily-pull's absolute worst-case 05:00 UTC). OR raise monthly timeout-minutes to 180.

findings.title: Checkout default fetch-depth (1) — shallow clone breaks any git-history-based logic
findings.description: Both workflows use `actions/checkout@v4` without specifying `fetch-depth`. Default is 1 (shallow). The retry-rebase logic at line 270 calls `git pull --rebase --autostash --strategy-option theirs origin main` — this fetches additional refs lazily so the rebase itself works, but any downstream script that introspects git history (commit count, blame, methodHistory git-log-based dedupe, etc.) will see only HEAD. The check-pull-stats.js, picks-regression-check.js, and walk-forward scripts may rely on file mtime instead, which works on shallow clones, so this is likely not currently triggering — but is a latent footgun.
findings.mechanism: actions/checkout@v4 defaults to fetch-depth: 1. Any node script doing `git log --since='4 weeks ago' -- snapshots/_manifest.json` would return only the current commit's manifest. Scripts using fs.statSync mtime are unaffected (mtime is set at checkout to commit time, not file commit time — actually mtime defaults to checkout time, breaking mtime-based time series).
findings.reproducer: Add `git log --oneline -- snapshots/_manifest.json | wc -l` to a step; observe it returns 1 regardless of actual history depth.
findings.impact: Any future script relying on git history will silently see only HEAD. Current scripts using mtime-of-file see all files with mtime = checkout time, making mtime-based 4-week-median trend analysis effectively meaningless.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `with: { fetch-depth: 0 }` to checkout step in both workflows. Also set `with: { persist-credentials: true }` explicitly (default but documented intent).

findings.title: Pipeline Health Check runs without continue-on-error — single per-script noise spike hard-fails entire pipeline mid-run
findings.description: Comment at line 158-162 says Pipeline Health Check is intentionally hard-fail. But it runs BEFORE earnings/historical/macro/walk-forward steps. If the health check trips (5% threshold on per-script failure rate from previous run state), the entire workflow exits with failure, skipping: earnings calendar, historical prices, macro regime, walk-forward performance, method effectiveness, methodology report, pick-diff, elliott-export, pull-stats, method-drift, diff-report, dashboard, archive, alert-state truncate, COMMIT, and Pages deploy.
findings.mechanism: A single bad day in `scripts/pipeline-health-check.js` (e.g. modes-report has 6% failure rate on a 2400-stock universe due to one transient Yahoo issue) hard-fails the workflow. The commit step is skipped entirely — none of today's snapshots, picks-history, methods-history land in main. picks-history vintage gap. methods-history vintage gap. Yet snapshots in ./snapshots/ might be perfectly fine.
findings.reproducer: Force any one per-script `_failures` count above 5% threshold; verify entire downstream pipeline including commit is skipped.
findings.impact: Single noisy run discards an entire day of useful pipeline output AND breaks vintage continuity for picks-history/methods-history (the exact thing comments at lines 132-133 say must not happen).
findings.suggested_fix: Move Pipeline Health Check to AFTER Commit Snapshots + Alert-State, so even if it trips, today's data is preserved. OR change to `continue-on-error: true` and add a final dedicated 'fail workflow if health-check tripped' step at the end.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-CI-002 — silent stale-manifest pass at coverage gate + hard-fail at health-check mid-run = worst of both worlds.

findings.title: Deploy to GitHub Pages uses orphan-init + force-push, losing all gh-pages history
findings.description: Deploy step does `git init -b gh-pages` inside _site/, commits, then `git push --force origin gh-pages`. Each run replaces the entire gh-pages branch with a single-commit orphan. Reasonable for static deploys, but: (a) no history of past reports on gh-pages (rollback impossible), (b) GitHub Pages cache invalidation may lag (force-push on an orphan can occasionally serve stale content for 60-120s), (c) `if: success()` gate combined with this means an intermittent failure in any step before Deploy results in yesterday's gh-pages persisting silently.
findings.mechanism: Each daily run: mkdir _site, copy files, git init -b gh-pages, commit, force-push. Previous gh-pages SHA is overwritten — no preserved history. Force-push on orphan means commit-SHA changes every run, defeating gh-pages caching heuristics.
findings.reproducer: Visit github.com/<org>/screener-data/commits/gh-pages — every entry is from yahoo-pull-bot with the same orphan parent.
findings.impact: (1) Cannot diff today's vs last week's deployed report on gh-pages. (2) If today's Deploy step fails silently (network blip during force-push — there's no retry), gh-pages stays at yesterday's content with no alert. The Notify on failure step only fires on `if: failure()` of the overall job; a single-line transient push retry would be a one-line fix.
findings.suggested_fix: Use `peaceiris/actions-gh-pages@v3` or `actions/deploy-pages@v4` (the latter pairs with `actions/upload-pages-artifact` and the workflow already declares `pages: write` + `id-token: write`). If keeping the manual approach, wrap `git push --force` in the same 3-retry loop used for main pushes.

findings.title: Secret DISCORD_WEBHOOK referenced in 5 places without existence check at workflow start
findings.description: DISCORD_WEBHOOK is consumed by detect-changes.js, picks-regression-check.js, pipeline-health-check.js, check-pull-stats.js, and the failure-notification step. The failure step at line 315 correctly checks `if [ -z "$DISCORD_WEBHOOK" ]`. The other 4 scripts each must implement the same check independently — if any doesn't, they crash/log misleadingly when the secret is missing on a fork or fresh checkout. FINNHUB_API_KEY is similar at line 61.
findings.mechanism: GitHub Actions injects `${{ secrets.X }}` as empty string when undefined. Node scripts that POST to an empty webhook URL get either DNS failure or POST to '' which throws TypeError.
findings.reproducer: Fork repo without secrets set, run workflow_dispatch.
findings.impact: Misleading error messages on fork/contributor runs; potential noisy stack traces in CI logs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a once-per-job step that warns about missing secrets, OR add a single env-validation script at top of job that exits cleanly with informative message.

findings.title: alert-state truncate step shell-injects $size into node -e string
findings.description: Line 256 embeds the bash variable $size into a node -e string concatenated via string template (`'+$size+'`). $size comes from `stat -c %s alert-state.json` so it's an integer in practice — but the pattern is fragile: any future change to derive $size from a non-numeric source (e.g. filename) would inject arbitrary JS. Also: the truncate step has no continue-on-error, but no error handling either — if node throws on parse, the workflow hard-fails before commit.
findings.mechanism: `'+$size+'` is shell-interpolated by bash before node runs. If $size contains a single quote or backtick, JS parse fails. Currently safe because stat output is always numeric.
findings.reproducer: Replace `stat -c %s` with `ls -la | awk '{print $5}'` (typical refactor) — adds extra spaces, breaks node parse.
findings.impact: Hard-fail of commit step on edge-case filenames or stat-format changes. Low because the current command is deterministic.
findings.suggested_fix: Pass $size as an environment variable to the node process and read it with `process.env.PREV_SIZE`. Wrap the entire conditional in error tolerance with `|| true` or add `continue-on-error: true`.

findings.title: Inconsistent retry-loop sleep between daily-pull and monthly-sec-xbrl push paths (5*i vs 5*i, but daily logs 'attempt $i' on success only)
findings.description: Both workflows use identical retry logic (3 attempts, sleep 5*i seconds, exit 1 on final failure). Monthly version is missing the explicit 'Push failed after 3 attempts' echo before exit 1 — a minor logging gap. Both share the same vulnerability described in F-CI-001.
findings.mechanism: Same as F-CI-001. Failure mode masked by `|| true` after `git rebase --abort`.
findings.impact: Slightly worse debugging UX on monthly failures — exit 1 with no preceding error message in step log.
findings.suggested_fix: Mirror daily-pull's explicit failure echo, OR (better) factor both retry loops into a shared composite action `.github/actions/git-push-retry`.

findings.title: Monthly SEC workflow lacks pipeline-health/coverage gate — silent partial pulls land in manifest
findings.description: monthly-sec-xbrl.yml's `Pull SEC XBRL` step has no continue-on-error AND no post-pull coverage gate. So a partial pull (job timeout at 90 min on a large universe) commits a partial manifest with no warning. Compare to daily-pull which has explicit Verify Pull Coverage gate.
findings.mechanism: If pull-sec-xbrl.js crashes after writing partial manifest entries, the commit step still runs and pushes the partial manifest to main. Next month, the pull may not realize cache is incomplete and silently reuses partial data.
findings.reproducer: Force pull-sec-xbrl.js to throw after processing 30% of tickers. Workflow exits non-zero so the commit step doesn't run — actually safe by accident. BUT if pull-sec-xbrl.js exits 0 with incomplete data (timeout caught via try/catch then graceful exit), no gate catches it.
findings.impact: Possible silent partial SEC manifest commits if pull-sec-xbrl.js handles its own timeout gracefully.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a coverage gate similar to daily-pull's Verify Pull Coverage between Pull SEC XBRL and Commit manifest.

findings.title: No npm cache configured in setup-node — every run downloads full dependency tree
findings.description: Both workflows' `actions/setup-node@v4` blocks omit `cache: 'npm'`. Each daily run does a fresh `npm ci` from scratch. For a Node project this typically adds 30-90s per run. Over a year of daily runs that's 3-9 hours of needless minute-billing.
findings.mechanism: Without `cache: 'npm'`, setup-node does not restore package-lock-based npm cache. npm ci fetches every tarball from registry.npmjs.org every run.
findings.reproducer: Compare 'Install dependencies' step duration before/after adding cache: 'npm'.
findings.impact: Performance only — adds ~1 min per daily run, ~30 min/month, ~6 hours/year of GitHub Actions minutes.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `cache: 'npm'` to both setup-node steps (requires package-lock.json at repo root — workflow already runs `npm ci` so it exists).

findings.title: Verify Pull Coverage manifest-fallback file-count uses the same threshold formula — fallback path can over-credit
findings.description: When manifest is empty/zero, the script falls back to `file_count` and assigns `ok=$file_count; total=$file_count`. The threshold then becomes `max(1800, floor($file_count * 0.4))` — which is always 40% of the count itself, i.e. always passes the 40% test trivially. Only the floor of 1800 actually gates. So a partial pull that produced 1801 .json files passes, even if the intended universe was 13000.
findings.mechanism: `min_ok = max(1800, floor(total * 0.4))`. If total = 1801 (fallback), min_ok = max(1800, 720) = 1800. ok = 1801 ≥ 1800 → pass. Even though the real universe was 13000.
findings.reproducer: Simulate timeout that leaves 1800 .json files + no/zero manifest. Verify Pull Coverage passes.
findings.impact: Gate is effectively just 'did we produce >=1800 json files' on the fallback path. 1800 of 13000 is 14% coverage — far below the intended 40%. Downstream report quality silently degrades.
findings.suggested_fix: In file-count fallback, derive `total` from `watchlist.json` length (it's already on disk), not from file_count. Then ok=file_count and the 40% threshold actually means 40% of intended universe.
findings.compound_hint: Compounds with F-CI-002 — stale-manifest path uses inflated yesterday's total, file-count fallback uses self-referencing total. Neither path correctly measures coverage of the intended universe.
